title: The Mountain I'd Already Decided I Couldn't Climb
summary: A day trekking Hoang Nguu Son (972m) near Nha Trang with the Zalo team — from the line of us winding up the slope to the summit marker, then down to the sea to look back up at what we'd just climbed. A reflection on the limits we quietly draw, the pace that actually carries a team, and why coming down is part of the climb.
author: Nguyen Le Phong
html:
The morning of the climb began the way most ordinary mornings do, with no sign that anything was about to change. There was no cinematic light, none of the feeling those final photos seem to promise. Just shoes on dirt, a bag settling onto my shoulders, the sun already up, and a line of us stepping onto the trail up Hoàng Ngưu Sơn. If I am honest, the loudest thing in my head at that point was not excitement. It was a small, familiar sentence I had said to myself many times before, in many other situations: I'm probably not built for this one.

Most of us carry a sentence like that. We meet a project, a role, a mountain, a hard conversation, and somewhere quietly we decide the result before taking a single step. We draw a line and put ourselves on the smaller side of it. The strange part is how reasonable it feels in the moment, and how rarely we go back to check whether the line was ever real.

This was a team trip — a Zalo trek — so the line on the slope was a line of colleagues, the same pale-blue shirts, the same bucket hats, numbers pinned to a few backpacks. That detail matters, because it changed what the climb was about. Alone, a mountain is a test of will. With a team, it becomes something gentler and harder at the same time: a question of how you move when other people are moving beside you.

Everyone has a first day

Before the trip, two lines from my older brother kept coming back to me. The first: do not put a ceiling over yourself too early. The second sounds almost too plain to mean anything — everyone has a first day of school. The older I get, the more I think the plain one is the stronger of the two. A lot of what we are afraid of is not actually beyond us. We have just forgotten that everyone starts as a beginner. A first trek. A first hard project. A first role that feels a size larger than the confidence we currently own.

So this is not really a story about conquering a mountain. It is closer to a story about walking past a line I had drawn for myself, one small and slightly tired step at a time — and noticing that I was not walking past it alone.

A team is a pace, not a race

In the middle of the climb, speed stops being interesting. Breath matters more. Water matters more. The ability to pause without feeling like you have failed matters more. There were stretches where I wanted to push faster, mostly to prove to myself that I was fine. The trail did not care about that part. It answers very plainly: rush now, and you pay for it on the steeper section above.

A team makes the lesson louder. A strong pace is not the fastest pace; it is the one the whole line can keep without leaving anyone behind or running anyone into the ground. You watch the people around you. Someone slows, so you slow. Someone goes quiet, so you offer water before they ask. Endurance turns out to be partly a social skill: not being careless with yourself, and not being careless with the people walking near you. I thought about that while remembering a VnExpress interview with Lê Hồng Minh, where mountains become a way to talk about trying things, testing limits, and lasting. What stayed with me was not the appetite for danger. It was the practical center of it: try, learn, keep a safety boundary, and when it gets too hard, sit down for a moment before you continue.

When the view opens, the problem changes size

There is a point on most trails where the view suddenly opens and your mind goes quiet. You see the color of the water far below, bright patches of sky, a town gone small, ridgelines folding into one another, and the route you have already walked. That kind of view does not solve anything. What it does is change the scale of the question. A problem that felt heavy at breakfast becomes one small part of a much wider landscape.

This is also why outdoor trips balance the rest of life for me. Work asks for focus, decisions, deadlines, and a hundred small pressures that rarely announce themselves loudly. The trail asks for less: breathe properly, keep walking, watch the path, protect the place you are passing through, and resist the urge to turn every beautiful thing into a task to be processed quickly. It is not an escape from work. It is a way to come back to work carrying a little less tension.

972 meters

The marker at the top reads Hoàng Ngưu Sơn, 972 m, with a faded red star and an old line that says Blue Eye, 2018. It is a small thing on a big rock, and yet a whole line of tired people had spent the morning walking toward exactly that.

The marker matters because it gives the day a visible point. But if you look only at the marker, you miss the quiet accumulation that made it possible: the preparation, the awkward first steps, the moment you nearly spent too much energy, the decision to rest, the patience to keep a steady pace. Most big changes in work and life arrive the same way. From the outside they look sudden. From the inside they were built slowly, in ordinary days that nobody photographed.

In software, the visible point is usually a release, a migration finished cleanly, a production fix handled without panic, or a new title. But the real story started much earlier: a clearer pull request, a calmer incident review, a better question asked in a meeting, a decision to learn something difficult before it became urgent. Progress is rarely loud while it is happening. It tends to sit quietly inside the days when you did one small thing a little better, or simply kept going before anyone could see a result.

Looking back up from the water

Coming down, the day folded toward the sea. We crossed a long stretch of open water in a cable car, the bay turning a deeper blue beneath us, and rode an open tram with the whole team packed in, everyone a little sunburnt and very happy. Going down is its own kind of attention — tired legs, looser talk, the relief of knowing the hard part is behind you.

Later I stood knee-deep in the shallow water of the bay, hands on my hips, and looked up at the green mountain we had come down from. That was the moment the whole day landed. A few hours earlier I had quietly decided the climb was probably beyond me. Now I was standing below it, looking up at a line I had already crossed. The mountain had not shrunk. I had simply moved to the other side of my own assumption, and from here I could finally see how small the line had been.

Coming down is part of the climb

After the summit, the rest of the trip did not feel like a separate reward. The slow morning by the pool, the sand, the quiet coffee, a loud team dinner under string lights — all of it was the part where the lesson settled into the body. If I had climbed and then jumped straight into the next task, I think I would only remember being tired and arriving. The calm afterward is what let me understand what I had actually walked through.

Endurance, I keep relearning, is not only the ability to keep pushing. It is also knowing how to come down: how to recover, how to return to ordinary life without carrying the whole mountain as tension in your shoulders. This is true at work too. There are seasons that ask you to push hard, and they are real. But if every goal turns into a chase with no resting point, the climbing stops meaning anything. The squirrel on the palm frond did not seem impressed by any of our medals, and somehow that felt exactly right.

I came back thinking less about conquering anything, and more about how easily I had drawn that first line in the morning — and how the day had quietly walked me past it. Everyone has a first day. Everyone has a first climb, a first project that feels too big, a first role bigger than today's confidence. The point is not to pretend the fear is gone. It is to keep enough humility to learn, enough honesty to know where you actually are, and enough courage to take the first step anyway.

The thought that stayed with me: a lot of our limits feel solid only because we have repeated them in our heads for a long time. A good journey does not erase them in one dramatic moment. It lets us test them carefully, widen them a little, rest when we need to, lean on the people walking beside us, and keep going with more trust in the quiet accumulation behind us.

If this reminds you of a trail, a project, or a season of learning where the change only made sense much later, I would be glad to hear how it went for you. Some of the most useful conversations start when we compare the ordinary steps, not just the finished photos.
